coredata.dc:title: Biomass to nanoparticles: Recent advances in the process and processing towards sustainability

coredata.dc:description: The application of nanomaterials in various sectors, including biomedical, energy storage and conversion, agriculture, and the environment, is fast gaining popularity and importance. The unique properties of nanomaterials promise a faster, greener and better future, particularly its unrivalled characteristics in surface functionality, electron mobility and photoluminescence effect. However, its synthesis, processing and development have received increasing attention in recent decades, particularly in the environmental aspect. This review critically summarises and discusses recent advances in the synthesis of nanomaterials from renewable sources like biomass and the development of various processes involved. Biomass, a green, sustainable and widely available precursor, is increasingly used for synthesising nanomaterials with lower environmental burden and costs and higher biocompatibility and quality. Recent progress has seen a series of zero-dimensional (quantum dots), one-dimensional (nanotubes), two-dimensional (nanofibres) and three-dimensional (nanocrystals) nanomaterials produced from various biomass and bio-feedstock. This review also discusses the synthesis strategies involved in the processing of biomass via the top-down and bottom-up approaches, as well as the mechanisms driving such conversion. Examples for each method are discussed illustrating the preparation, synthesis and the resultant nanomaterial quality. The potential of biomass-derived nanomaterials is increasingly highlighted in various studies which promotes a transition towards greener nanomaterials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of nanomaterials in various sectors, including biomedical, energy storage and conversion, agriculture, and the environment, is fast gaining popularity and importance. The unique properties of nanomaterials promise a faster, greener and better future, particularly its unrivalled characteristics in surface functionality, electron mobility and photoluminescence effect. However, its synthesis, processing and development have received increasing attention in recent decades, particularly in the environmental aspect. This review critically summarises and discusses recent advances in the synthesis of nanomaterials from renewable sources like biomass and the development of various processes involved. Biomass, a green, sustainable and widely available precursor, is increasingly used for synthesising nanomaterials with lower environmental burden and costs and higher biocompatibility and quality. Recent progress has seen a series of zero-dimensional (quantum dots), one-dimensional (nanotubes), two-dimensional (nanofibres) and three-dimensional (nanocrystals) nanomaterials produced from various biomass and bio-feedstock. This review also discusses the synthesis strategies involved in the processing of biomass via the top-down and bottom-up approaches, as well as the mechanisms driving such conversion. Examples for each method are discussed illustrating the preparation, synthesis and the resultant nanomaterial quality. The potential of biomass-derived nanomaterials is increasingly highlighted in various studies which promotes a transition towards greener nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials and bulk materials are two main categories of particles, and their application depends heavily on their respective properties. Bulk materials are defined as particles larger than 100 nm in all dimensions, and it is more common to the public than nanomaterials where the size is less than 100 nm in at least one of its dimensions [1]. Nanomaterials can be further categorised into (1) zero-dimensional nanomaterials such as quantum dots, (2) one-dimensional nanomaterials such as nanotubes and nanorods, (3) two-dimensional nanomaterials such as nanofilms and nanofibres, and (4) three-dimensional nanomaterials such as nanocomposite and core/shell nanoparticles [2].

The size of nanomaterials widens their application in fields where conventional materials are unreachable. Quantum dots, metal nanomaterials, carbon and silica-based nanomaterials with excellent photoluminescent properties are often used in the biomedical field [3,4]. Moreover, nanomaterials exhibit different properties depending on their size and shape [124], allowing them to be used as energy conversion devices, energy storage devices, catayst, absorption materials, and agricultural purposes [5,6,125]. Metal nanomaterials such as iron oxides were used for wastewater treatment due to their excellent adsorption capacity [7]. Semiconductor nanomaterials such as lead selenide quantum dots with great dispersion within polymer resulted in a large polymer interface area and were used as a solar device for wider solar spectrum absorption [8].

The increasing usage of nanomaterials in various industries, especially in the final product that involves direct usage by consumers, has been accompanied by the rising public concern about the environmental impact and health-related concern of the nanomaterials [9]. In China, National Nanotechnology Standardization Technical Committee (NSTC) is responsible for reviewing the safety aspects, including handling, usage, and disposal of nanomaterials [10]. It collects several existing policies and regulations, each governing its aspects. In the European Union, nanomaterial inclusion in the existing regulations ensures accurate handling and proper notification for the usage of products accessible by the public [11]. Similar approaches are observed in countries like the United Kingdom and the United States [12].

The toxicity nature of precursors when used in the biomedical field and the side effects when spent nanomaterials released to the environment are often discussed by researchers and diverted the research interest to source for biomass precursors and their synthesis methods [13,14].

Biomass-derived nanomaterials are widely applied in energy storage, such as supercapacitors and batteries. Liu et al. [15] synthesised mesoporous carbon from waste coffee grounds through catalytic carbonisation and alkali activation. The application in high-performance supercapacitor electrodes was explored. Due to the 3D porous structure and large meso-/macropores volume, the large surface area allows charge storage capacity to be maximised with quick ions transport rate. Thi Thanh Dang et al. [16] synthesised biomimetic mesoporous cobalt ferrite/carbon nanoflake materials and evaluated their electrochemical properties for application in lithium-ion battery anodes. Besides applications in energy storage, biomass-derived nanomaterials could be utilised in energy conversion [126]. Hoa et al. [17] reviewed fuel cells fed with biomass-based fuel. Biomass electricity is an optimum answer for energy due to its acceptable cost and effective conversion. Different biomass-based fuels include carbon-based nanomaterials, cellulose & cellulosic biomass, starch, alginate, bio-ethanol, bio-hydrogen, etc.

Furthermore, biomedical applications of biomass-derived nanomaterials such as bioimaging, biosensors and biomarkers are significant. Wang et al. [18] synthesised graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from rice husks suitable for biomedical applications due to their great biocompatibility. Bioimaging HeLa cells using GQDs synthesised was attempted, resulting in stable and reproducible luminescence from HeLa cells/GQDs. Besides bioimaging, biomass-derived nanomaterials could be applied in the biomedicine field. Using the hydrothermal method, Liu et al. [19] synthesised nanospheres from xylose as a carbon source and F127 as a template. Small mesoporous hydrothermal nanospheres (SMHN) were produced with a diameter below 100 nm and high loading capacity, allowing cellular delivery enhancement. Furthermore, Huang et al. [20] utilised by-products in the bio-refinery process, wheat straw and bamboo residues as starting materials for the synthesis of CQDs by one-pot hydrothermal method. The application of CQDs synthesised in bioimaging was explored. In vitro cytotoxicity of CQDs was tested using an assay with L929 fibroblasts, and cell viability >90% showed good biocompatibility. For bioimaging application, photoluminescence of CQDs was tested, and cells incubated with CQDs exhibited blue and red fluorescence. Hence, the experiments showed that the synthesised CQDs were excellent in bioimaging.

Moreover, biomass-derived nanomaterials could be applied in the biosensor and biomedical fields. Cai et al. [21] attempted synthesising fluorescently labelled starch nanocrystal (FL-SNC) from waxy maise. FL-SNC was biocompatible with cells. With the participation of active hydroxyls, fluorescent poly-hydroxyl nanoparticles could be potentially produced and widely used as biosensors and biomarkers. Furthermore, biomass-derived nanomaterials could be applied in the environment. Bhujel et al. [22] synthesised biomass-derived silver decorated graphene (AgGr) from onion for voltammetric detection of p-nitrophenol (PNP). AgGr composites act as a chemical sensor to detect PNP due to their high toxicity.

This review examines and compiles technologies for synthesising nanomaterials using biomass as the source, as shown in Fig. 1 . The objective is to review methods for directly synthesising nanomaterials from different biomass sources. The mechanism of each method is explained, and examples are given for each method with brief explanations on the experimental set-up (materials involved and conditions) and the results obtained (diameter and other properties). All examples included are summarised in Table 1 .

Electrospinning produces polymeric nanomaterials using electrostatic forces via electrospinning set-up, consisting of a high-voltage power supply, solution source, and ground collector [23], as shown in Fig. 2 . The solvent is chosen based on the type of polymer (i.e. hydrophilic or non-hydrophilic) used to produce the polymer solution, and later it was used to produce nanofibres [24]. The typical solvent includes alcohol, dichloromethane, chloroform, dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), hexafluoro-isopropanol (HFIP), and trifluoroethanol [25]. Two main types of electrospinning are needle-based and needleless electrospinning. The needle-based set-up has a voltage supplier providing 15 to 25 kV voltage between the spinnerets and ground collector. This induces forming Taylor cone, which later develops into a liquid jet of the polymer when electrostatic forces surpass the surface tension and accumulate at the collector [23]. Needle-less set-up eliminates the spinnerets and replaces them with magnet and bilayer system (i.e., polymer solution and ferromagnetic suspension), which vibrates the free surface of the bilayer system and forms sites of jetting, and is collected at the counter electrode (the collector) [26].

Polyhydroxybutyrate obtained from microalgae was used as one of the precursors for the polymer solution to produce electrospun nanofibres [27]. Polyhydroxybutyrate (35 m/v%), phenolic compounds (1 m/v%), sodium chloride (2.4 m/v%) and chloroform (61.6 m/v%) were mixed for 16 h to produce the polymer solution required for electrospinning [28]. In particular, polyhydroxybutyrate nanofibre with an average diameter of 810 ± 85 nm was produced under 15 kV and at a feed rate of 150 µL/h. The melting temperature recorded was 241.9 °C with a maximum degradation temperature of 287.0 °C. Moreover, it showed a tensile strength of 2.9 MPa and an elongation break of 71.5% [27].

Lignocellulosic biomass-derived lignin, the most abundant biopolymer, produced phosphorus-functionalised electrospun carbon fibre [29]. Phosphoric acid (85 wt%), lignin (7.5 wt%) and ethanol (7.5 wt%) were blended for 12 h until reaching the viscosity 350–450 cPs before the solution was used for electrospinning [30]. During the electrospinning process, the inner tip fed the solution at 3 cm3 h−1. In contrast, the outer tip was feeding pure ethanol at 0.3 cm3 h−1, and the electric potential applied was 24 kV with subsequent stabilisation via heat treatment up to 200 °C. Phosphorus functionalised carbon fibre was produced with an average diameter ranging between 400 and 1000 nm with a tensile strength of 250 MPa. Moreover, an increase in the surface oxygen content (from 4 to 8 wt%) was observed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

Chitin, another biopolymer derived from crab and shrimp shells, was used to produce electrospun chitosan nanofibres [31]. Chitosan powder (6 wt%) was added to trifluoroacetic acid and dichloromethane (70:30 v/v) together with 5.4 (v/v%) glutaraldehyde solution (25 wt% water) to form a polymer solution for electrospinning. The solution was fed at 0.1 mm min−1 with an electric potential of 18 kV during electrospinning, and nanofibres were produced with an average diameter of 91 nm [32].

Ethylcellulose, a derivative of cellulose, was used as one of the precursors for synthesising gold nanoparticles-loaded polyvinylpyrrolidone/ethylcellulose coaxial electrospun nanofibres [33]. 0.8 g polyvinylpyrrolidone and 1 ml aqueous gold nanoparticles were added to 10 mL ethanol to make the first solution system fed through the outer tip. The second solution system, to be fed through the inner tip, was prepared by dissolving 1.4 g ethylcellulose in a 10 mL mixture of dichloromethane and dimethylacetamide (1:1, v/v). The feed ratio of the inner tip to the outer tip was manipulated from 0.5:1, 1:1, and 1.5:1 with an electric potential of 12 kV between the spinneret and collector. The resulting nanofibres had tensile strength ranging from 1360 to 2260 MPa depending on the inner to outer tip feed ratio.

In summary, feed rate and the electric potential play a vital role in forming nanofibres and avoiding the formation of beaded nanofibres. Polymer feed rate differs between polymer solutions and typically ranges between 150 and 3000 µL/h to maintain stable jet cone and fibres diameter. Electric potential ranges between 15 and 25 kV differ between polymers due to different charge repulsions within the polymer jet [34]. Electric potential beyond a critical point, which differs between polymer solutions, would increase the diameter of nanofibres, which later result in bead formation. The advantage of electrospinning includes ease of control and inexpensive set-up, whereas the drawback includes the use of toxic solvent and limited controllability of its pore structure [35,36].

Pyrolysis is a thermochemical process often used in biomass pre-treatment [127,128] where the properties of resultant biomass char are largely affected by the lignocellulosic composition of biomass [129,130]. Moreover, pyrolysis is also used to degrade biomass to the nanoscale [37], as shown in Fig. 3 . Before pyrolysis, the furnace is purged with noble gas (i.e., argon gas) to ensure the system is oxygen-free. The furnace temperature is usually maintained between 800 and 1500 °C [38,131]. Pyrolysis techniques available for nanomaterial synthesis include fixed bed pyrolysis [39], spray pyrolysis [40], fluidised bed pyrolysis [41], and pyrolysis in thermal plasma [42]. Fixed bed pyrolysis is the most common type, and it can be carried out in single or two-stage reactors, which can be either vertical or horizontal [39,43]. Spray pyrolysis enables continuous pyrolysis where gaseous precursors and catalysts are introduced to the reaction zone [40]. Fluidised bed pyrolysis is usually carried out in a stainless-steel reactor with gas precursors and fluidising agents (H2 and N2) introduced from the bottom through the solid catalyst [44]. Pyrolysis is thermal plasma that uses plasma as a microwave irradiator to achieve the heating effect, leading to the breakdown of precursors to the nanoscale [42].

Crude biomass such as grass was used as a precursor for synthesising carbogenic nanoparticles via pyrolysis [45]. The grass was shredded and pyrolysed in an oven for 4 h at 400 °C. The nanoparticles displayed a red shift in photoluminescence when the excitation wavelength was increased from 350 to 600 nm. Based on biomass, the yield of nanoparticles with a diameter between 2 and 5 nm was 30 wt%. All samples exhibited high colloidal stability where the aqueous dispersion remained clear without agglomeration after incubation in a harsh environment.

Porous carbon nanoparticles were synthesised by pyrolysis using waste pods of Caesalpinia Sappan as a biomass source [46]. Shredded waste pods of 62 µm were introduced to a tube furnace with N2 gas flowing at 150 cm3/min from 400 to 1000 °C. At 1000 °C, the nanoparticles had a carbon content of 91% and a surface area of 995.6 m2/g, contributed by the porous spherical nanostructure that is only available at a temperature beyond 800 °C. Moreover, a diameter between 20 and 30 nm is found at 1000 °C, increasing as the pyrolysis temperature decreases. Furthermore, the nanoparticles showed excellent thermal stability and no apparent weight loss when exposed to temperatures up to 1000 °C.

Rice husk was used as a biomass source to synthesise silica nanoparticles via the pyrolysis process [47]. In a muffle furnace, the rice husk was pretreated with hydrochloric acid and later pyrolysed at 700 °C for 2 h to form silica nanoparticles. The nanoparticles were ultrasonicated and dried before being subjected to pyrolysis at 800 °C for up to 8 h to form a semicrystalline porous silica framework [48]. The resulting porous nanoparticle demonstrated a diameter of 25–30 nm with a surface area of 164 m2/g.

Lignin nanoparticles and precipitated lignin were used as biomass precursors, in powdered form, to synthesise graphene-like porous carbon via pyrolysis [49]. Temperature elevation was performed at three stages: (1) 30 °C to 180 °C, (2) 180 °C to 450 °C, and (3) 450 °C to 1100 °C with temperature increment of 2 °C/min at each stage, and the temperature was maintained at the final temperature of each stage for 1 h, 1 h, and 3 h, respectively. The resulting graphene-like porous carbon from lignin nanoparticles and precipitated lignin showed a specific surface area of 62.26 and 67.95 m2/g and a total pore volume of 0.061 and 0.063 cm3/g, respectively.

To summarise, pyrolysis temperature plays a major part in defining the morphology and particle size of the nanomaterials. The porous structure that increases the nanoparticle's surface area has higher absorption capability and is determined under increased pyrolysis temperature (800–1000 °C). Higher pyrolysis temperature shows smaller particle size as more energy is provided in the form of heat to dissociate larger precursors. With sufficient energy provided at high temperatures, volatile substances are removed, and the synthesised product would be rich in carbon content.

Hydrothermal carbonisation, also known as wet pyrolysis, utilises water in the biomass or water supplied to the system to produce carbon nanoparticles in an aqueous solution within a closed reactor under subcritical conditions [50], as shown in Fig. 4 . Subcritical water has a lower dielectric constant than water at ambient conditions, resulting in weaker hydrogen bonding producing an acid environment, benefiting acid catalysed reaction, with high H+ concentration without acid addition [51]. Carbon sources can be obtained from biomass such as glucose, xylose, maltose, sucrose, amylopectin, and starch [52]. Hydrothermal carbonisation can be performed at high temperatures (between 300 and 800 °C) and low temperatures (between 180 and 300 °C) [53]. Multiwalled carbon nanotubes can be synthesised via high-temperature hydrothermal carbonisation at 800°C and an autogenous pressure of 1000 bar. In contrast, monodispersed colloidal spheres can be transformed into the nanoporous structure via low-temperature hydrothermal carbonisation at 250 °C [54,55].

Glucose can be used as a precursor for synthesising carbon nanoparticles via the hydrothermal process. The synthesis of carbon nanoparticles via a solvent extraction and solvent assisted routes has been reported [56]. Both routes involved dissolving 10 g glucose in 300 mL distilled water, which was then placed in a stainless-steel reactor (500 ml) and treated hydrothermally at 200 °C for 1, 2 and 4 h. In the solvent extraction hydrothermal carbonisation, the organic solvent is added to the solution before the hydrothermal process. In solvent-assisted hydrothermal carbonisation, the organic solvent is added before the hydrothermal process. Butyl acetate is an organic solvent that showed the highest yield of 2.82 and 14.11 in solvent extraction and solvent assisted routes.

Moreover, the solvent extraction route led to carbon nanospheres with an average diameter ranging between 34 nm and 122 nm. In contrast, solvent assisted route resulted in carbon nanospheres with an average diameter between 80 and 275 nm. Photoluminescence at an excitation wavelength between 300 and 480 nm showed fluorescence peaks at 345 and 495 nm for carbon nanosphere synthesised from both routes.

Biomass such as pine needles and oak leaves are used as a source for the synthesis of mesoporous carbon nanoparticles via the hydrothermal process [57]. Biomass, water, and citric acid were added to form a solution and placed in an autoclave at 200 °C for 16 h. Carbon nanoparticles formed from soft biomass without extended crystalline scaffold were between 20 and 200 nm in size and between 10 and 100 nm in pore size. Carbon nanoparticles formed from these biomasses illustrated a smaller diameter than glucose and starch. Polar components in such biomass allow carbon nanoparticles to have hydrophilic surface chemistry. Moreover, the nanoparticles showed effective gas sorption pores of 12 nm and an area of 34 m2/g.

Overall, the temperature at which the precursors have hydrothermally treated influences the porosity and the size of the nanoparticles. Higher temperature would result in nanoparticles with smaller diameters due to the energy provided for the de-agglomeration of large particles into nanoparticles.

Ultrasonication utilises sound waves to synthesise nanoparticles from biomass by reducing the particle size to the nanoscale [58], as shown in Fig. 5 . Typically, ultrasound waves between 20 and 10 MHz are applied to the medium containing the precursors of the nanoparticles [59,132]. Ultrasound waves, during their travel, cause compression and rarefaction of the liquid medium, leading to the formation of tiny gas bubbles (cavities) that eventually collapse and release energy at high local temperature and pressure [60,61]. The energy released is sufficiently large to disintegrate biomass to the nanoscale level [62,63]. Moreover, longer sonication time produced radicals due to unnecessary oxidation and resulted in radical-induced polymerisation, which reverses the process; hence it is important to maintain sonication time below 60 min [59].

Cellulose fibrils were isolated from cellulose fibres via ultrasonication. Cellulose materials include pure cellulose fibre, microcrystalline cellulose, and pulp fibre [64]. Soaked cellulose was treated with ultrasound waves at 20 kHz with power ranging between 600 and 1200 W for up to 60 min [65]. The separated fibrils showed a diameter of 20–30 nm under atomic force microscopy, and better fibrillation efficiency was observed with increasing power, resulting in water temperature as high as 91 °C [64,65].

Brown rice husk was used to synthesise silica nanoparticles via ultrasonication. Ash of brown rice husk was mixed with HCl to remove metal ions before sonication [66]. The solution was then treated at 35 kHz with 240 W for 50 min. The silica nanoparticle showed an increasing diameter from 5 to 40 nm. A reduction in pore size diameter from 4.97 to 4.11 nm while an increase in pore volume from 0.2349 to 0.2558 cm3g−1 were observed as the sonication time increased, indicating higher porosity.

Fluorescent nano-biomass dots were synthesised using soybean as biomass precursor [58]. Treated soybean presents a dark yellow solution, which was processed to obtain nano-biomass dots with an average diameter of 2.4 nm. Nano-biomass dots demonstrated tuneable emission through different excitation wavelengths from 320 to 520 nm. Moreover, the dots showed high thermal stability where their fluorescence experience minimal reduction at temperatures as high as 80 °C. Photostability was tested by prolonged UV illumination, and emission intensity was maintained above 90%.

Soft wood kraft lignin was used as a biomass source for lignin nanoparticle synthesis via ultrasonication [62]. Aqueous lignin suspension was treated at 20 kHz and 130 W for 2, 4, and 6 h. Aqueous polyurethane solution (35 w/w%) was added to the synthesised lignin nanoparticle to form a polyurethane-based nanocomposite. In lignin nanoparticles, prolonged exposure to ultrasound waves has resulted in a smaller diameter, ranging between 10 and 50 nm. UV-vis absorption spectra with a peak at 280 nm showed the presence of phenolic groups in the nanoparticle. The nanoparticle was found to be thermally stable after 6 h of sonication at 200 °C, whereas the polyurethane-based nanocomposite was found to achieve higher thermal stability at 400 °C. Furthermore, polyurethane-based nanocomposite was found to have tensile strength as high as 6.9 MPa. In another study, aqueous lignin suspension was sonicated for 1 h at 20 kHz with 600 W and exhibited a similar diameter range between 10 and 50 nm [67].

In general, it can be deduced that sonication power and frequency are correlated to the formation of cavities within the sonicated medium. Cavities, upon collapses, releases a vast amount of energy to deagglomerate the precursors to nanomaterials of smaller diameter. Cavities are better sustained at low frequency, 20 – 40 kHz, and an increase in the sonication power would allow the cavities to be sustained at a higher frequency. A higher nanoparticle volume is typically observed with increased sample exposure time to ultrasound, suggesting that sonication time is the key to nanoparticle formation.

Chemical vapour deposition (CVD), where the solid material is deposited from vapour by a chemical reaction occurring in or on the area of the heated substrate surface [68]. The detailed mechanism is demonstrated in

Fig. 6 . CVD is the most commonly utilised thin-film deposition method to synthesise nanomaterials, especially carbon nanotubes [69]. CVD technique is acknowledged as a widespread method due to its advantages, including high process flexibility, controlled reaction parameters, adaptability of large-scale processing, and promising growth rate [70]. The applications of CVD include nanofabrication of semiconductor chemical sensors [68], nanosized coatings [71], two-dimensional nanosheets from different materials [72] such as graphene [72], etc.

Fathy [73] attempted to synthesise carbon nanotubes from rice straw using the CVD method. Rice straw was pretreated by alkali-acid reagents (NaOH and H2SO4). The ferrocene and nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O) catalyst was added to the pretreated rice straw, heated, filtrated, washed, and dried. Powdered rice straw (RS-H) was then obtained after filtration and drying. Furthermore, RS-H was added to an ethanol solution containing ferrocene and sonicated. The mixture was then charged into an autoclave tube and heated. Then, the mixture was hydrothermally treated in distilled water, separated and dried. The resulting sample was subjected to the CVD process in the presence of camphor. Carbon nanotubes were synthesised in diameters from ∼22 to 66 nm.

Basheer et al. [74] utilised biomass powder-activated carbon (bio-PAC) that was synthesised from date palm fibre [75] to generate hybrid carbon nanotubes (CNTs). Fe catalyst was added in acetone and mixed with bio-PAC. The mixture was sonicated until evaporation of acetone. Bio-PAC/Fe produced was then dried and calcinated under inert gas (purified N2). Bio-PAC/Fe was placed in a ceramic boat with a CVD reaction tube. The CVD reactor was cooled to room temperature using purified N2, and after the completion of the reaction, the grown sample was collected. CNTs growth was carried out by reduction under H2, where C2H2 acted as a carbon source and was mixed with H2. The results showed that hybrid CNTs were produced with diameters ranged between 23.02 and 27.93 nm.

Besides, biomass-based biogas (particularly methane) shows potential as a carbon precursor in synthesising carbon nanotubes [76]. Khavarian & Mohamed [77] studied biogas reforming over multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) using the CVD method. MWCNTs were directly grown on the nanocomposite catalyst Co-Mo/MgO with biogas methane (CH4) during the catalytic CVD process. The CVD process was conducted in a continuous rotary reactor at 825 °C with the feed gas containing CH4 and N2 at a certain volumetric ratio. The results indicated MWCNTs with average diameters ranging from 10 to 30 nm.

As deduced from the examples above, CVD processes employ different parameters such as temperature (range of 800-1000°C), pressure and carbon precursors used. Higher temperature tends to produce the larger size of nanoproducts [78]. The CVD set-up allows large-scale nanomaterials that fulfil the industrial requirement of consistency and quantity. Hence, CVD is the most followed method for industrial-scale synthesis.

Flame processes are the most widely utilised for manufacturing nanoparticles commercially. A flame deposition is carried out by combusting precursors until vaporisation with enough fuel, and the vaporised precursors are deposited on the surface while in contact with the surface. The mechanism of this method is shown in Fig. 7. The widespread of this technology is due to its simplicity as it is a one-step process and possesses “non-moving parts” [79]. Flame synthesised materials include metal oxides [80], non-oxides such as carbon [81], metals (iron) [82], etc.

Flame synthesised nanoparticles from biomass have been investigated. Using corn-based cooking oil as an organic carbon precursor, Chang et al. [83] synthesised carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) as cationic dye adsorbents through the flame deposition method. Corn oil was filled into a glass petri dish, and wicks were immersed with the exclusion of end tails. The fire was then ignited at the exposed end of the wick and covered using a beaker with a slight gap at the bottom for airflow. The corn oil was burned as fuel in the combustion process, and CNPs were deposited as flame vapour while in contact with the glass surfaces of the beaker. After corn oil was used up, the flame was extinguished and deposited CNPs were collected. The results indicated that the size of primary CNPs generated ranged from 35 to 60 nm.

Besides, Kshirsagar et al. [84] synthesised CNPs from multiple green precursors using the flame deposition method. Organic carbon precursors include almond flames, mustard oil, burnt almond char and clarified butter. CNPs were synthesised by burning the listed organic precursors using the flame soot method. The collection of soot was on a clean metallic surface. The precursors were then completely combusted and deposited over a metallic surface. CNPs formed were collected by scratching and were transferred into a centrifuge tube for purification. The results confirmed that CNPs formed with a particle size of 15–25 nm.

Based on the examples above, the parameter of the flame deposition method varies depending on the employed carbon precursor as the starting material. The advantages of this method include a considerably easy process as it is one-step synthesis, green synthesis with no harsh chemicals involved, and a riskless process.

Laser ablation utilises laser as an energy source for the ablation of solid materials. The term “ablation” is defined as the removal of surface atoms that involves apart from a single photon process (breaking of chemical bonds) and includes multiphoton excitation (thermal evaporation). The purity of nanoparticles generated from laser ablation mainly depends on the purity of the target material and ambient media (gas or liquid) without contamination. For the laser ablation process, the laser is first focused on the surface of solid target material in the ambient media (gas or liquid). A substantial amount of energy from the laser is concentrated on the surface. The temperature of the irradiated spot speedily increases and leads to the vaporisation of the target material. The evaporated atoms and clusters then collide with the surrounding molecules, resulting in the excitation of the electron state. Light is emitted while electrons and ions are produced during the excitation of electron start. A laser-induced plasma plume is formed. Nanoparticles are formed by supersaturated vapour nucleation at the edge of laser plume when vaporised materials are speedily quenched from extremely high to room temperature [85]. The mechanism of laser ablation is illustrated in Fig. 8 . Typical nanomaterials obtained from laser ablation are semiconductor quantum dots such as ZnSe and CdS [86], SnO2 [87], metal nanoparticles that include gold [87,88], silver [88], metal oxides including iron oxide [89,90] and ZnO [90].

Using biomass as a source of nanoparticles through laser ablation, Tavangar et al. [91] showed a technique synthesising green functional carbonaceous 3-dimensional micro or nanostructures from rice husks and wheat straws. Rice husks and wheat straws were washed and dried overnight. Dried materials were then ground into powder and coated on Si substrates. A single-point femtosecond laser was focused and irradiated on the specimens under ambient conditions (air). The detailed laser parameters are listed in Table 1. SEM was carried out with morphological analyses using various laser parameters, such as pulse energy and several pulses. The results confirmed that the fabricated nanofibrous structures are comparatively uniform 3-D structures at micro and nano sizes.

Besides, Wang et al. [92] derived photoluminescent carbon quantum dots from soybean. Soybean residual was first subjected to pre-treatment to be carbonised using the hydrothermal method. Ground soybean residual was mixed with H2SO4 and stirred ultrasonically. The mixture was then heated, filtered, and dried. Hydrothermal carbonised carbon dots produced were then subjected to high-temperature annealing. The annealed carbon dots were heated, pressed, and subjected to laser ablation in an aqueous NH4OH solution. The detailed laser parameters are listed in Table 1. The results indicated that soybean-derived nanoparticles are polygonal and have an average size of 9.59 ± 0.99 nm.

The parameters involved are laser parameters such as wavelengths, power, pulse width, spot size, etc. The size of nanoparticles shows increment with an increase in wavelength [93]. The preparation of this method is simple as the equipment completely controls the process. Easy tuning of laser parameters could be carried out. Moreover, this method is capable of good particle size control and good monodispersity in the synthesised nanoproducts, as shown by the examples above.

Magnesiothermic reduction is a thermic method for synthesising silicon nanoparticles from silica nanoparticles. Silica nanoparticles are magnesiothermic reduced to silicon nanoparticles by adding magnesium as a reducing agent. Magnesiothermic reduction is favoured for producing silicon from silica as its operating temperature is lower (500–950 °C) [94], compared to another thermic reduction method (i.e. carbothermal reduction at 2000 °C), which allows preservation of porous nanostructure during silicon formation [95]. The mechanism of this reaction is shown in Eqs. (1) and (2) below. Magnesiothermic reduction of rice husks with high silica content [96] to produce silicon nanoparticles has been explored.

Liu et al. [97] attempted to synthesise silicon nanostructure from rice husks in a green and large-scale process for a high-performance Li-ion battery anode. Rice husks (RHs) were first converted to silicon dioxide/silica (SiO2) by burning RHs in the air. The product was then leached using HCl. After leaching, leached rice husks were washed with deionised water, dried, and heated in air at 700 °C at a ramp rate of 5 °C min−1. Magnesium (Mg) was added as a reducing agent to produce nano-Si; the reaction was performed by mixing Mg and SiO2 in powder while heating at 650 °C. After the reaction, HCl was added to powder form to remove unwanted products such as MgO, Mg2Si and unreacted Mg. Si nanoparticles synthesised from the above process were about 22 nm.

Besides, Sekar and Lee [98] derived luminescent silicon nanocrystals from rice husks using the magnesiothermic reduction method. Three rice husks (biomass sticky rice husks, red rice husks and brown rice husks) were attempted. RHs were first carbonised and converted into RH ashes. RH ashes were leached using HCl and rinsed with deionised water. Leached RH ashes were calcinated at 700 °C to obtain SiO2 nanopowders. Mg powder was mixed with SiO2 powder for reduction purposes, and the mixture was annealed at 700 °C under Argon atmosphere in a tube furnace. The product was post-treated by HCl to remove MgO formed and hydrofluoric acid (HF) to eliminate residual SiO2. The morphological results showed the size of nanocrystals from three types of RHs ranged from 15 to 50 nm.

Unlike other methods, magnesiothermic reduction is a specific method that synthesises silicon nanoparticles by chemical reaction. The parameters that affect the process include temperature at 500–950 °C. A recent study indicated that higher temperatures within the range mentioned above lead to a decrement in surface area and pore volume but an increment in sizing [99]. The post-treatment of this method should be concerned with removing by-products to obtain a high purity product. Moreover, this method is considerably simple in terms of the mechanism involved. Hence, this method is considered straightforward and easy to control.

Bio-based nanoparticles such as starch and cellulose nanoparticles are investigated due to their abundance and renewability in the source. Starch and cellulose can be obtained from plants [100,101]. Modifications of native starch are required due to its functional limitations for industrial applications. Natural limitations could be improved significantly by modifications through chemical methods [102]. The most common chemical method is acid hydrolysis [100], a typical method for synthesising cellulose nanocrystals [101]. The mechanism of this method is demonstrated in Fig. 9 . Examples of hydrolysis of starch nanoparticles and cellulose nanoproducts are shown below.

Saeng-on and Aht-Ong [103] synthesised starch nanocrystals using mild acid hydrolysis. Unripened bananas were utilised as the source of starch. Banana starch was extracted based on the earlier reported method [104]. Unripened bananas were peeled, cut into small cubes, immersed in NaOH solution, and soaked. The mixture obtained was sieved and precipitated. The obtained precipitate was degreased by washing with distilled water and decanted for residual starch to precipitate. Decanting and washing were repeated until no impurities were noted in washing water. The starch precipitate was filtered and dried to form a starch powder. Starch nanocrystals (SNC) were prepared using the Angellier et al. [105] method with slight adjustments. The starch powder was dissolved in acid solution (H2SO4/HCl) under constant stirring. Hydrolysis was triggered, and the suspension formed was neutralised by consecutive dialysis. Homogenizer was used for redissolving to avoid aggregation. The mixture was then freeze-dried to obtain loose SNC powder. The results showed 47.13% crystallinity with about 30–70 nm.

Cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) could be produced using acid hydrolysis from pinecone flower waste [106]. Pinecone powder was mixed with NaOH and heated under magnetic stirring. The precipitate was filtered and washed until a neutral pH. The product was then bleached using sodium hypochlorite solution, and the mixture was stirred and filtered. Dried cellulose formed was mixed with H2SO4 and stirred at 45 °C. Cold distilled water was added to stop the hydrolysis reaction. The mixture formed was washed and centrifuged until the pH reached neutral. The precipitate of nanocellulose formed at the bottom was collected and dried. The nanocrystals resulted in high crystallinity with 15 nm wide and 185 nm length. Moreover, Huang et al. [107] synthesised cellulose nanofibril (CNF) composite with water-soluble lignin (WSL). WSL was obtained by adsorption and desorption processes on prehydrolysate with chemicals. CNF was synthesised by the NaClO-NaBr-TEMPO oxidation method from bleached kraft pulp. WSL was added to homogenized CNF suspension to form a CNF-WSL composite. From the characterization results, WSL obtained showed a diameter of 6–12 nm. CNF-WSL composite formed showed an increment of tensile strength with excellent UV protection and antibacterial properties.

Besides, starch nanoparticles were also synthesised using mild alkali hydrolysis and ultrasonication from horse chestnut (HS), water chestnut (WS) and lotus stem (LS) by Ahmad et al. [108]. Samples of HS, WS and LS were peeled or de-hulled, reduced in size and ground with water in a blender. Extraction of starch was carried out by the alkaline steeping method [109]. Starch solutions produced were preheated in NaOH solution and continuously stirred. The starch slurry was then obtained and sonicated. The solution formed was co-precipitated by ethanol under continuous magnetic stirring. The precipitate formed was centrifuged for collection and freeze-dried using a freeze dryer. The hydrodynamic nanoparticles produced from three different sources ranged from 420 to 606 nm [108].

To conclude, the hydrolysis method focuses on synthesising bio-nanomaterials such as starch and cellulose. Chemicals involved in the process are mild acid and mild alkali. Parameters in this method include concentrations of acid/alkali used and carbon precursor used. This method typically synthesises 3-D nanocrystals. Moreover, the size of nanoproducts synthesised is considerably large (>100 nm) due to the cellulose/starch molecules involved in the synthesis process.

The sol-gel method, known as chemical solution deposition, requires several stages sequenced from hydrolysis and polycondensation, gelation, aging, drying, densification, and crystallisation [110]. The hydrolysis of the precursor was carried out in acidic or alkaline conditions [111]. Mechanism of sol-gel method is shown in Fig. 10 . Materials suitable for the sol-gel method are metal and metal oxide [112], silica [113], non-silica oxides such as TiO2, ZnO, etc. [114], and ceramic materials such as nitrides and carbides [115]. Sol-gel applies to several sectors, such as the preparation of catalyst [116], thin-film and coatings [117], derivation of phosphors [118], scaffolds [119], etc.

Using biomass as the source, silica nanoparticles were synthesised by Le et al. [120] from Vietnamese rice husk (RH) using the sol-gel method. RH was first pretreated with HCl and H2SO4 and combusted in a muffle furnace to remove integrated hydrocarbons. Rice husk ash (RHA) containing silica was leached using HCl and H2SO4, followed by filtration and washing with distilled water until pH reached 7. To prepare sodium silicate solution, sodium hydroxide solution was added to pretreated RHA and boiled. The heating process led to the dissolution of silica and the formation of sodium silicate solution. For the synthesis of silica nanoparticles, surfactant (i.e. cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), cetyl amine (CA), etc.) was dissolved in water/butanol solvent. RHA-derived sodium silicate solution was slowly added into the solvent and stirred. To trigger the hydrolysis-condensation reaction, H2SO4 was added slowly into the suspension to adjust the pH to 4. The gel mixture was aged for a set period. The aged silica gel was dissolved in butanol and washed using distilled water repeatedly. Nanosilica generated was then calcinated to remove surfactant. The results indicated that the size of nano silica was at 3 nm.

Besides silica nanoparticles, Mohd Anuar and Andas [121] synthesised silver (Ag), cobalt (Co) and silver-cobalt (Ag-Co) nanoparticles using different metal precursors using the sol-gel method. RH was first pretreated using nitric acid (HNO3) for a long period and dried. Sodium silicate solution was produced by mixing pretreated RH with sodium hydroxide solution. Glucose was added as a reducing agent into sodium silicate and mixed homogeneously. The solution was titrated with nitric acid containing either AgNO3, Co(NO3)2.6H2O or a mixture to correspondingly produce Ag-NP, Co-NP, or AgCo-NP. After titration, the gel solution was aged at room temperature, then centrifuged and repeatedly washed with water and twice with acetone. The gel produced was suction filtered, dried, and ground. The size of NPs generated ranged from 3.18 to 3.57 nm.

To summarise, the sol-gel method is a chemical synthesis method that involves alkali, acid, surfactant, and organic solvent from pre-treatment to post-treatment. The method is considered complicated due to multiple steps involving complex chemicals, which lead to a long process time. Parameters in the process differ in the utilised chemicals, such as solvent, heating conditions and precursor selection. Particle sizing could be adjusted by the parameters mentioned before [99]. It should be noted that this method tends to produce very fine nanoproducts, which is about 3 nm based on the examples above.

Rising concern on the health, safety, and environmental aspect of nanomaterials and their synthesis has diverted attention to biomass-derived nanomaterials. In this review, ways of obtaining biomass-derived nanomaterials, including electrospinning, pyrolysis, hydrothermal, ultrasonication, chemical vapour deposition, flame deposition, laser ablation, magnesiothermic reduction, hydrolysis, and sol-gel methods have been critically discussed. The synthesis method is the decisive factor for forming the final nanomaterial regardless of the initial biomass utilised. Thermal, chemical, and hybrid syntheses are three widely used and reported methods. Three-dimensional nanocrystals are commonly produced via magnesiothermic reduction, whilst two-dimensional nanofibres are generally synthesised via electrospinning. One-directional nanotubes are produced via chemical vapour deposition, and zero-dimensional quantum dots and nanoparticles are synthesised via pyrolysis, flame deposition, ultrasonication, hydrothermal, sol-gel, hydrolysis, and laser ablation. The choice of methods is pivotal in determining the resulting quality and the environmental and economic factors, and hence should be made with an informed decision.

This review paper is one of the very first few comprehensive reviews, if not the first, to critically summarise and discuss the growing trend in exploring alternative precursors, in addition to the more commonly used chemicals and fossil resources, for nanoparticle synthesis. It is a comprehensive review on understanding the potential of various biomass feedstock and the associated synthesis approach suited for each material. The advantages and drawbacks of each starting material are discussed, together with their potential applications.
